recipes.title: Poutine
recipes.description: Quebec's iconic dish: hot crispy fries topped with squeaky cheese curds and smothered in rich brown gravy.
steps: Cut potatoes into 1 cm thick fries. Soak in cold water 30 minutes, then drain and pat thoroughly dry.
Heat oil to 150°C (300°F) and blanch fries for 5 minutes until tender but pale. Drain and rest.
For the gravy: melt butter, whisk in flour, and cook 2 minutes to make a roux.
Slowly whisk in both broths along with Worcestershire and vinegar. Simmer 8 minutes until thickened. Season with pepper.
Increase oil to 190°C (375°F) and fry potatoes again for 3–4 minutes until deeply golden and crisp.
Drain fries, salt immediately, and pile into deep bowls or plates.
Scatter cheese curds over hot fries, then ladle hot gravy over to melt the curds slightly. Serve immediately.

recipes.title: Tourtière
recipes.description: Quebec's traditional spiced meat pie of pork and beef encased in flaky pastry — a Réveillon Christmas Eve staple.
steps: Make pastry: cut cold fat into flour and salt until pea-sized, mix in ice water until just clumping. Divide in two, flatten, chill 1 hour.
Sauté onion in a heavy pan until soft, add garlic, then both meats and brown.
Stir in cinnamon, cloves, nutmeg, savory, salt, pepper, and broth.
Add diced potato and simmer 25 minutes uncovered until liquid evaporates and the mixture is thick. Cool completely.
Roll one disc of pastry to line a 23 cm pie pan. Fill with cooled meat mixture.
Roll second disc and lay on top, sealing and crimping edges. Cut steam vents and brush with beaten egg.
Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 20 minutes, then reduce to 175°C (350°F) and bake 30 more minutes until deeply golden. Rest 15 minutes before serving.

recipes.title: Butter Tart
recipes.description: Canadian buttery, gooey-centered pastry tarts of brown sugar, butter, and egg in a flaky shell — runny or firm-fillinged, but always sweet.
steps: Cut cold butter into flour and salt until pea-sized, then add ice water just until dough comes together. Chill 1 hour.
Roll dough on a floured surface to 4 mm thick. Cut 12 cm rounds and press into a 12-cup muffin tin.
Whisk brown sugar, corn syrup, melted butter, eggs, vanilla, and vinegar until smooth.
If using, scatter raisins or pecans into the bottom of each tart shell.
Pour filling into shells, filling no more than two-thirds full to prevent overflow.
Bake at 200°C (400°F) for 15 minutes, then reduce to 175°C (350°F) and bake 8 more minutes until edges are golden but the center still wobbles.
Cool in the tin 10 minutes — the filling will set as it cools — then transfer to a rack.

recipes.title: Nanaimo Bar
recipes.description: British Columbia's no-bake three-layer dessert: chocolate-coconut crumb base, custard buttercream middle, glossy chocolate top.
steps: Line a 20×20 cm pan with parchment paper.
For the base: melt butter with sugar and cocoa over a double boiler. Whisk in beaten egg and cook 2 minutes until thickened.
Off heat, stir in graham crumbs, coconut, and almonds. Press firmly into the pan and chill 30 minutes.
For the middle: cream softened butter, then beat in powdered sugar, custard powder, and milk until smooth and fluffy.
Spread custard layer evenly over the base. Chill 30 minutes until firm.
For the top: gently melt dark chocolate with butter over low heat until smooth. Cool slightly.
Pour over the chilled middle layer, smooth, and chill 1 hour. Cut with a hot knife into squares.

recipes.title: Montreal Smoked Meat
recipes.description: Hand-cut, peppery, brined and smoked beef brisket, piled on rye with mustard — Montreal's defining sandwich.
steps: Combine kosher salt, curing salt, sugar, and half the spices for the cure. Rub all over brisket.
Place brisket in a sealed bag and refrigerate 7 days, flipping daily.
Rinse brisket thoroughly under cold water and pat dry. Press the remaining black pepper, coriander, and mustard seeds into the surface.
Smoke at 110°C (225°F) over hardwood for 4 hours until a deep bark forms.
Transfer to a steamer or a covered rack over simmering water for 3 hours until extremely tender (internal 95°C).
Rest 30 minutes, then carve hand-sliced (not machine) across the grain.
Pile generously on rye bread slathered with yellow mustard. Serve with pickles.

recipes.title: Bannock
recipes.description: Indigenous-inspired Canadian quick bread that can be pan-fried, baked, or cooked over a campfire — simple, hearty, and adaptable.
steps: Whisk flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar in a large bowl.
Stir in melted butter, then gradually add warm water or milk, mixing into a soft, slightly sticky dough.
Knead lightly on a floured surface for 30 seconds — do not over-knead.
For pan bread: shape into a 2 cm thick disc, place in a greased cast-iron skillet, and bake at 200°C (400°F) for 25 minutes until golden and hollow-sounding when tapped.
For fry bread: divide dough into 8 portions and flatten each into rounds about 1 cm thick.
Heat oil to 175°C (350°F) and fry rounds for 2 minutes per side until puffed and deeply golden.
Drain fry bread on paper towels. Serve warm with butter, jam, soup, or savory toppings.

recipes.title: Pea Soup
recipes.description: French Canadian habitant pea soup of dried yellow split peas slow-cooked with salt pork, onions, and herbs — a winter mainstay.

ingredients.name: salt pork or smoked ham hock

steps: Rinse split peas thoroughly under cold water and drain. No need to soak.
In a large pot, place split peas, salt pork or ham hock, water, bay leaves, savory, and thyme. Bring to a boil and skim foam.
Reduce heat to low, cover partially, and simmer 90 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking.
Add onion, carrot, celery, and garlic; continue simmering 30 minutes more until peas are completely broken down and creamy.
Remove the salt pork or ham hock, shred the meat off, and return it to the pot.
Discard bay leaves, season generously with black pepper and salt to taste.
Stir in chopped parsley and serve hot with crusty bread.

recipes.title: BeaverTails
recipes.description: Canadian fried whole-wheat dough pastries shaped like a beaver's tail, dusted with cinnamon sugar — Ottawa's Rideau Canal classic.
steps: Dissolve yeast and sugar in warm milk; let foam 10 minutes.
Add egg, melted butter, vanilla, salt, and both flours. Mix into a soft dough.
Knead 8 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover and let rise 1 hour until doubled.
Punch down and divide into 8 portions. Roll each into a long oval about 5 mm thick — like a beaver's tail.
Heat oil to 180°C (350°F).
Fry each tail for 1 minute per side until puffed and deeply golden. Drain briefly on paper towels.
Mix cinnamon and sugar in a shallow dish. Toss hot tails in cinnamon sugar to coat. Serve immediately.

recipes.title: Donair
recipes.description: Halifax's signature pita sandwich of spiced spit-roasted beef, tomato, onion, and a sweet condensed-milk garlic sauce.
steps: Knead ground beef with garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, paprika, pepper, salt, and cayenne for 5 minutes until tacky and smooth.
Press tightly into a loaf pan, eliminating air pockets. Bake at 175°C (350°F) for 1 hour until firm.
Cool slightly, then slice the loaf as thinly as possible.
Crisp the sliced beef in a hot dry skillet for 2 minutes per side until edges are caramelized.
For the sauce: whisk condensed milk with vinegar and garlic powder until thickened — it sets in seconds.
Warm pita breads briefly. Pile crispy donair meat on each pita.
Top with tomato, onion, and a generous drizzle of donair sauce. Wrap and eat.

recipes.title: Peameal Bacon
recipes.description: Ontario's brined boneless pork loin rolled in cornmeal, sliced thick, and pan-fried — the heart of Toronto's beloved peameal sandwich.
steps: Combine water, kosher salt, brown sugar, curing salt, garlic, bay leaves, and peppercorns in a pot. Bring to a simmer to dissolve, then cool completely.
Submerge pork loin fully in the cold brine and refrigerate 4 days.
Remove pork, rinse very well, and pat dry.
Spread cornmeal on a tray and roll the pork loin firmly to coat all sides in a thick crust.
Wrap tightly and refrigerate 24 hours so the cornmeal adheres.
Slice into 1 cm rounds and pan-fry in butter over medium heat 3 minutes per side until golden and just cooked through.
Serve in soft kaiser rolls with mustard for the classic peameal bacon sandwich.

recipes.title: Saskatoon Berry Pie
recipes.description: Prairie Canadian pie filled with sweet-tart Saskatoon berries (similar to blueberry, with an almond note) in a flaky double crust.
steps: Cut cold butter into flour and salt until pea-sized. Add ice water and form into two flat discs. Chill 1 hour.
In a saucepan, combine 250 g of berries with 100 ml water and bring to a simmer for 5 minutes — this releases their flavor.
Whisk cornstarch with 3 tbsp cold water and stir into the simmering berries until thickened. Cool.
Toss the cooked berry mixture with the remaining fresh berries, sugar, lemon juice, and cinnamon.
Roll one disc to line a 23 cm pie pan. Pour in the filling and dot with cubed butter.
Roll the second disc and lay over the top. Crimp edges, cut steam vents, brush with egg, and sprinkle with coarse sugar.
Bake at 220°C (425°F) for 15 minutes, then reduce to 180°C (350°F) and bake 40 more minutes until crust is golden and filling bubbles. Cool 2 hours before slicing.

recipes.title: Maple Taffy
recipes.description: Quebec sugar-shack treat: hot maple syrup poured over fresh snow, set briefly, then twirled onto a stick into chewy maple candy.

ingredients.name: clean fresh snow or shaved ice

steps: Pack a long, shallow tray with clean fresh snow or finely shaved ice. Press it firm and flat. Keep outdoors or in the freezer.
Pour maple syrup into a heavy saucepan with a candy thermometer.
Bring to a boil over medium heat, watching carefully — maple syrup foams aggressively.
Cook without stirring until the temperature reaches 112–115°C (234–239°F), the soft-ball stage.
Remove from heat and let foam subside for 30 seconds.
Pour the hot syrup in long ribbons over the cold snow. Wait 15–30 seconds for it to firm slightly but stay pliable.
Press a wooden stick into one end of each ribbon and twirl it up to gather the taffy. Eat immediately.
